But as a merchant, it acts as a reseller and/or wholesaler of room inventory in bulk. This means that it buys room to be added to its inventory (often at a discounted rate) and resells them to customers. Suppose a hotel room costs $3,000 per night. EXPE may negotiate a net rate of $2,500 per room if it buys 100 rooms before reselling it to $2,800 to customers. With that, it will earn $30,000. This benefits the three parties while helping EXPE avoid direct competition with the hotel.

4. Its Bullish Outlook Justifies The Uptrend. Because of its robust Q3 2025 performance, EXPE increased its revenue outlook from 3-5% to 6-7%. So, it’s not surprising that its stock price also jumped.

With that, I also had to update my projection. My new revenue high and low estimates using the new estimated range of EXPE are $15.528B and $14.412B, respectively. Meanwhile, I still set COGS (77.30%) and operating expenses (10.30%) as a percentage of revenue based on their trends in the past four quarters. Their value also increased in line with the revenues. Lastly, depreciation and capex remained the same since I still used the straight-line method.

With these numbers, you can see that EXPE still sustains its growth. Costs and expenses are still manageable, so it also maintains a double-digit operating margin. Considering this, sustained uptrend in the stock price is still justifiable.

To assess it further, we can use the EV/Sales model. Currently, the company’s EV is $34.8B, so my EV/Revenue high and low are 2.24x and 2.40x, respectively. Meanwhile, the reported EV/Revenue FWD is 2.39x, which is already very close to the EV/Revenue low. This suggests that the market stays conservative since investors value the stock at the lower end of the guidance. The FWD stock valuation is based on the expectation that the revenue will more or less increase by 6.2-6.4%. I derived it by dividing EV by EV/Revenue FWD, which is $14.560B before getting the percentage change from the 2024 revenue of $13.691B. So, if the revenue increased by 7%, there is a big chance that the price will breach $300 again.

Risks

As a hotel, inflation is still a risk factor for EXPE. Currently, the US inflation is 2.7%, which is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. This means that prices are still increasing today. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs since they raise domestic prices and affect the value of domestic currencies. Purchasing power and discretionary spending may be affected, and tourism demand is not an exception.

Protection

The recent rate cut by the Fed may offset the impact of inflation. This can boost borrower and investor confidence and spur economic activity. So, employment and wages may benefit. This may translate into improved purchasing power. If it does not happen, the policy easing cycle may at least benefit credit card holders and applicants. This can lower borrowing costs, which makes credit card spending more accessible and more affordable. So, it may also support travel spending. Lastly, this may help EXPE improve its liquidity since lower rates can also lead to lower repricing of EXPE’s variable-rate borrowings.

Technicals

EXPE’s technicals have weakened in the past two weeks. Its price dipped after breaking above $300. Despite this, the stock still shows a possibility of a rebound since it hasn’t broken below the $230 support yet. Also, the price is still coincided with the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is still at part with the average in the past 50 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which shows that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

The current trend can be explained by MACD and RSI. The MACD line is still trending downwards below the signal line as selling volume increases. Meanwhile, the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume recently. However, RSI shows that the downtrend is not a confirmation of a bullish reversal yet. The dip was due to the recent overbuying that prompted profit-taking. Now, momentum appears to have normalized as the strength index of the price starts to stabilize. Note that the price trend has become more manageable in the past four trading days. With that, the recent price dip may have opened new buying opportunities.

Conclusion

Expedia Group, Inc. remains a solid stock with its robust performance, supported by its strategic business model, brand popularity, and operational efficiency. It also maintains a strong positioning with its high liquidity levels, which can sustain its operating capacity and dividends. Valuation also stays reasonable even after the huge price jump. Technicals still show bullish signals with new buying opportunities after the recent profit-taking.

data.title: Rogers Sugar: Revisiting The Thesis As LEAP Is Delayed
data.description:
If you've been following my coverage on Rogers Sugar (RSI:CA), you probably noticed the stock hasn't really done much since I slapped a 'Hold' on it last August.

From where I sit, the story hasn't really changed much.

Maple keeps compounding nicely, Project LEAP should add another leg of growth into FY 2027, and the story still looks very defensive to me.

It keeps being exactly like a bond-like equity—slow growth, steady dividends, predictable risk. If you own it, there's really not much to do here other than clip the dividend and wait for LEAP to start showing up in the numbers.

Still, after five months, I think it makes sense to go back to the thesis and put it through a tougher stress test.

A few loose ends are still hanging out there, especially the weak sugar backdrop in Canada and the delay in opening the Eastern refinery.

A Nearly Sweet Quarter

Looking at the quarter, the Sugar segment once again weighed on sales—down about 3% year over year to $322.6 million—even as profitability moved the other way, with adjusted EBITDA up roughly 3% to $39.4 million.

It's basically the same dynamic we've been seeing for a while.

Maple keeps gaining traction, but margins are still thinner, and it remains a relatively small part of the overall business. Sugar, on the other hand, is softer on growth volume, but it still carries higher margins and is already in a mature phase of its cycle.

Let’s start by breaking the numbers down by segment, beginning with Sugar.

Sugar revenue slipped about 5%, and the segment’s share of total sales edged down to roughly 80%, versus nearly 82% last year. Volumes followed the same direction, easing around 4% to roughly 195,000 metric tons.

I see at least three reasons behind this decline in sugar volumes in Canada:

  1. They lost two clients in Western Canada;
  2. One client ran into a one-off structural issue (management didn’t disclose the details on the call); and
  3. Customers that are operating normally have been dialing back orders since other inputs (like cocoa) have become very expensive, forcing them to cut production.

And this last point is an interesting one to delve into a bit.

Cocoa is sitting near $4,201 per ton today—still pricey compared to the pre-2023 world, but nowhere near the insanity of the 2024–2025 peak.

That’s pushing producers toward either shrinkflation or outright price increases, which almost always end up hurting volumes.

As regions like Ivory Coast and Ghana (responsible for ~70% of global production) recover from a weak harvest, the trend is for supply to normalize.

But I really don’t think that tells the whole story.

Canadians, Americans, and frankly most of the world are dialing back on excess sugar and leaning more toward natural sweeteners like honey and maple syrup.

Over the long run, that shows up in declining per-capita sugar consumption.

Despite the softer top line, Sugar’s adjusted EBITDA still managed to grow 2.4% year-over-year.

But this didn't happen by chance.

My reading is that they're focusing on higher-return contracts and pushing selective pricing, which makes the volume drag more of a long-tail issue than something that really hurts near term.

Looking at Maple—the other side of the story, as I mentioned earlier—this is where Rogers’ growth really sits.

Maple now represents roughly 20% of revenue and about 14% of adjusted EBITDA. Growth was strong on both lines, with revenue up 5.7% and adjusted EBITDA up 8.2%, supported by stronger global demand for natural sweeteners (as I told you before).

On a full-year FY 2025 basis, both revenue and adjusted EBITDA increased.

Dividends Are Safe, but Not Like They Say

One topic we have a lot to talk about is dividends, of course.

That's the heart of the thesis.

Management keeps pointing out that the payout ratio is moving in the right direction (from roughly 85% in FY 2023 down to about 64% in FY 2025).

A near-6% yield looks sustainable, but I arrive at that conclusion using a different set of metrics and, frankly, a more conservative lens.

For example, in FY 2025, Rogers returned approximately $46.1 million in dividends (as I accurately predicted in the last write-up). Since Capex is temporarily high due to the LEAP, FCF was $32.7 million, failing to cover this.

On my numbers, FY 2026 OCF lands around $65.5 million. I'm leaning on the $1.31 billion revenue consensus, a 10% EBITDA margin, and roughly a 50% cash conversion.

Assuming Capex doesn’t really roll off yet (which lines up with management’s FY 2027 normalization comment), FCF here looks close to the trough.

Just as they had a cash deficit of just over $10 million in FY 2025, they will likely see something similar in FY 2026.

But can they do this comfortably?

With long-term debt at $361.1 million, essentially no relevant cash on the balance sheet, and projected EBITDA in the $131–$144 million range, leverage screens at roughly 2.5x Debt/EBITDA. That looks manageable, especially if Capex actually comes down after FY 2027, which management has clearly signaled.

Just reminding you, I don’t expect any dividend increases until the LEAP rolls off and FCF gets a significant lift.

There is one catch, though—not a big one, but worth flagging.

In the latest press release, management is guiding to roughly $117 million of Capex in FY 2026. At that level, FCF would slip to just under negative $50 million, which in turn could push long-term debt closer to $400 million, versus the roughly $370 million I was modeling in the prior write-up.

Extra Colors About LEAP

As I mentioned earlier, the Eastern Canada plant has been pushed to the end of the first half of FY 2027, which frees up both additional capacity to negotiate with customers and a supply route into the West.

It's been some time since my last piece on Rogers Sugar, so it's worth revisiting what the story looks like in concrete financial terms.

With an updated perspective in hand, let’s recap the setup.

To start with, the Montreal refinery will add at least 100,000 metric tons of incremental refined sugar capacity, along with the logistics needed to serve additional markets.

We don’t have perfect disclosure on Rogers’ installed refining capacity, but if current utilization is roughly 80% (about 781,500 metric tons out of 976,800), the incremental 100,000 tons would represent at least a 10.2% increase in effective capacity.

Pretty strong, if you ask me.

To make this happen, Rogers needs to invest roughly $280 to $300 million in total, with about $173 million already spent across FY 2024 and FY 2025.

In constant terms (almost back-of-the-envelope math), they would achieve an incremental EBITDA of ~$12.1 million, giving us a ROIC of almost 5%.

The lift doesn't look huge initially, but factoring in reduced logistics costs in Eastern Canada, along with incremental gross margin and geographic leverage, we could be looking at $20–$25 million of incremental EBITDA (ROIC of ~7.5%, approximately).

Since the EBITDA to OCF conversion is ~50% here, we're talking about something very close to $12.5 million in cash every year, which alone would cover a good portion of the annual maintenance Capex.

If the post-LEAP numbers play out anywhere close to expectations, FY 2027 EBITDA could be around $180 million, with OCF close to $90 million. Capex has normalized to something like $30 million by then; FCF starts to look more like $60 million.

At that point, leverage would settle around 2.2x Debt/EBITDA, assuming debt is still close to the $400 million mark, which finally allows the company some room to grow the dividend.

Is an Upside in Sight?

I still don’t think the smartest way to value Rogers Sugar is by looking at next year alone and a normalized view in FY 2027 makes much more sense.

For the setup, we can assume roughly $400 million in long-term debt (around $390 million net), post-LEAP EBITDA between $175 and $185 million ($180 million midpoint), about 130 million shares, and an EBITDA multiple in the 6.5x–9.5x range based on peers like Südzucker (SUEZY), Adecoagro (AGRO), Tate & Lyle (TATYY), and Associated British Foods (ASBFY).

At that level, EBITDA would imply a 13.5–14% margin on FY 2027 consensus revenue, which appears reasonable once synergies and cost savings are taken into account.

I’ll also be using a discount rate of roughly 6.2%, based on flat dividends and a beta of about 0.63.

Putting it all together, the updated valuation points to a price target range with a midpoint right around $7 in two years.

That implies roughly 12.9% price upside by FY 2027, plus a mid-single-digit dividend yield along the way.

Funny that this matches Wall Street's price target perfectly.

That said, I'm going to keep the 'Hold' here.

This price target already bakes in successful execution on the LEAP and a continuation of the Maple and Sugar dynamics. At today’s levels, I don’t see a near-term catalyst that would justify a 'Buy' for income investors, mainly because upside really doesn't open up until FY 2027 or even FY 2028.

If the stock were to drift down toward ~$5 and offer about a 25% margin of safety, I’d start getting interested.

Otherwise, it’s a hold and wait for the LEAP to play out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Waystar: Platform With A Strong Distribution And Technical Integration Moat
data.description:
Investment Summary

My recommendation for Waystar Holding Corp. (WAY) is a buy rating. I believe WAY has a scalable and defensible business model, supported by its distribution and technical moat. With AI improving the product rather than displacing it, I believe it should continue to easily grow above industry growth rates, allowing it to deliver mid-teens growth over the next several years.

Business Overview

WAY offers a cloud-based Revenue Cycle Management (RCM) platform that automates the movement of money from patient to provider. Essentially, it connects providers to insurance companies and facilitates the payment process. So far, WAY has scaled to a size where it processes >6 billion insurance transactions every year, covering ~50% of the US patient population. It also integrates with more than 500 electronic health record and practice management systems. In terms of economic model, ~55% of revenue comes from recurring subscriptions, with the rest tied to volume-based fees linked to the gross value of claims processed. As for customer base, WAY serves a massive client base, including 17 of the top 20 US health systems.

Distribution And Technical Moat

WAY’s moat, I believe, can be seen in the ambulatory segment (~70% of total revenue). Like software companies selling to hundreds and thousands of SMEs being a tough thing to do and replicate at scale, it’s the same for WAY. For WAY, it is selling into thousands of small, fragmented physician groups. Unlike selling into a massive healthcare system, in which one would need to go through a complex, multi-year RFP process, but at the end of the day, one signature from a C-suite gets you millions in revenue; selling to the ambulatory market (to entities like independent physician groups, clinics, and labs) is the exact opposite. It is as basic as you can imagine, which is to go door-to-door, in hopes that these entities would be willing to adopt your solution. For a competitor to replicate WAY’s footprint, they would need to burn through tons of capital and resources because of this inefficient customer acquisition cost (CAC) dynamic. They would have to firstly deploy a massive sales force to go door-to-door and hope that they can scale up to where WAY is, while WAY is already ahead and leading.

Aside from this distribution moat, there is also a technical moat to note. A lot of these smaller entities have no tech expertise. Their focus is to serve patients and ensure operational aspects of things move along nicely. Hence, they do not use standard IT systems like Epic or Cerner (mostly used by large hospitals). Instead, they use a disparate set of Practice Management (PM) systems that work for their specific needs (for example, dermatologists use different software than physical therapists). In this context, it doesn’t make sense to create 1 platform that can serve every one of these niche markets. WAY did a smart thing here by creating one platform, but one that has integrations with more than 500 of these disparate IT systems. For a competitor to challenge WAY’s position, they cannot just build a better platform; they have to technically replicate all these integrations. As simple as it sounds, this is a very slow process, as one would need to negotiate, come to an agreement on the terms and conditions, etc.

This, then, creates incredible stickiness for WAY. Once these smaller entities integrate WAY into their workflow to handle everything (things like patient verification to claims, etc.), it makes their life so much easier operationally. The thought of switching out would just mean unnecessary pain (unless WAY stops working well). From there, WAY has a “direct line” to these smaller entities that competitors do not have, which makes its go-to-market much more efficient. Once WAY has a good product to sell (or one that it has R&D), it just needs to contact these providers, say that there is a new product that could make their life easier, and WAY could potentially bundle this at $x price. If this is something that the provider needs, there is a good chance of an upselling success, which helps WAY capture more wallet share and increase stickiness (and retention).

AI Is A Tailwind

I believe one major misconception that the market has is that AI is going to displace the need for RCM software. My view is that AI on its own does not solve the underlying complexity. At the end of the day, how well AI functions are dependent on the data it feeds on (or uses). You cannot just go to ChatGPT and paste a few PDF files and hope that things work. In the context of RCM (especially against thousands of small providers), one would need a platform/workflow that already connects providers, payers, and data systems. With this, an agentic AI can use all the flowing data points to execute its instructions.

That workflow/platform layer is what WAY provides, which also means WAY is in a very good position to be the one leveraging these AI capabilities. This is what management has been doing. They are rolling out agentic AI tools to handle tasks like drafting appeal letters for denied claims, which helps their users to reduce manual effort and speeds up cash collection. Viewed from this angle, AI is actually a tailwind for WAY since it makes the platform better and gives WAY justification to raise prices. We have operating data to show that AI is not displacing WAY too. According to management, they are sustaining win rates of ~80% in competitive deals (source: JPM 2026 healthcare conference).

Valuation

Wells Fargo estimated the TAM for healthcare payment software to reach a size of $20 billion in 2027, which, I think, is fair since growth should track closely to overall domestic healthcare spending, which is about ~5% CAGR. This gives us the base to forecast WAY’s growth.

Assuming that industry growth is ~5%, we can back into the historical spread, which is about ~10% a year (FY25 revenue growth is guided by management). Holding that 10% for the next few years, I got to ~15% revenue growth, bringing total revenue to ~$1.44 billion in FY27. Management also implicitly guided for an FY25 adj. net margin of ~25%, which I have held the same for FY26 and FY27, a conservative assumption, as I assumed WAY would reinvest all incremental profits for growth. For this, business that is growing at mid-teens, I compared it against another peer within the healthcare sector, Doximity (DOCS), which grows at low teens, has a ~40% adj. net margin, and trades at 24x NTM PE. WAY’s growth is higher, but margins are much lower, and it also has a higher leverage ratio. As such, I thought attaching a lower multiple makes more sense. Assuming it's 20x NTM PE, my share price target is ~$38.

Risk

If a similar data breach (like the Change Healthcare Breach incident) happen at WAY, it could cause severe reputational damage, so much that providers churn out of the platform as they do not want to take the risk. There is also the inherent risk that any abrupt changes in CMS reimbursement rules or federal interoperability standards may force WAY to redesign their platforms in a huge manner, which in turn hurts margin in the near term as they work to rearchitect the platform.

Conclusion

My view for WAY is a buy rating. I believe WAY has both a distribution and a technical integration moat, which puts it in a good position to continue growing, as it already has the scale. I also think that AI should be seen as something that enhances WAY’s platform rather than a threat. Assuming WAY continues to grow at mid-teens and valuation re-rates slightly higher to 20x NTM PE, I believe there is attractive upside to the stock.
